messages.content:
Please select the right answer.

 a-Hos, quos video volitare in foro, quos stare ad curiam, quos etiam in senatum venire, qui nitent unguentis, qui fulgent purpura, mallem secum suos milites eduxisset.
 b-Hos, quos video volitare in foro, quos stare ad curiam, quos etiam in senatus venire, qui nitent unguentis, qui fulgent purpura, mallem secum suos milites eduxisset.
 c-Hos, quos video volitare foro, quos stare ad curiam, quos etiam in senatum venire, qui nitent unguentis, qui fulgent purpura, mallem secum suos milites eduxisset.
 d- Hos, quos video volitare in foro, qui stare ad curiam, quos etiam in senatum venire, qui nitent unguentis, qui fulgent purpura, mallem secum suos milites eduxisset.

The correct answer is:

a-Hos, quos video volitare in foro, quos stare ad curiam, quos etiam in senatum venire, qui nitent unguentis, qui fulgent purpura, mallem secum suos milites eduxisset.

This Latin sentence has the correct usage of "in foro" (in the forum), "ad curiam" (to the court), and "in senatum" (into the senate), which fits the context of the places where people are seen. It also maintains consistent relative pronoun use ("quos" for the accusative plural masculine and "qui" for the nominative plural masculine), which is congruent with the actions the people are doing (flying, standing, coming) and their descriptions (shining with oils, gleaming with purple).

Please select the right answer.

A- Patrimonia sua profuderunt, fortunas suas obligaverunt; res eos iam prident deseruit, fides nuper deficere coepit; eadem tamen illa, quae erat in abundantia, lubido permanet.
 B-Patrimonia sua profuderunt, fortunas suas obligaverunt; res eos iam pridem deseruit, fides nuper deficere coepit; eadem tamen illa, quae erat in abundantia, libido permanet.
 C-Patrimonia sua profuderunt, fortunas suas obligaverunt; res eos iam pridem deseruit, fides nuper deficere coepit; eadem tamen illa, quae erat in abundantia, lubido permanet.
 d-Patrimonia sua profuderunt, fortunas suas obligaverunt; res eos iam pridem deseruit, fides nuper deciphere coepit; eadem tamen illa, quae erat in abundantia, lubido permanet.

The correct answer is:

B- Patrimonia sua profuderunt, fortunas suas obligaverunt; res eos iam pridem deseruit, fides nuper deficere coepit; eadem tamen illa, quae erat in abundantia, libido permanet.

This Latin sentence correctly uses "pridem" (meaning "a long time ago") and "deficere" (to fail, to start to fail), as well as the correct spelling of "libido" (desire, whim).

Please select the right answer.

 A-Quem ad modum autem ilis carere poterunt, his praesertim iam noctibus?
 B-Quem ad modum autem illis carere poterunt, hic praesertim iam noctibus?
C- Quem ad modum autem illis carere poterunt, his praesertim iam noctibus?
D Quem ad modum autem illis carere poterunt, praesertim iam noctibus?

The correct answer is:

C- Quem ad modum autem illis carere poterunt, his praesertim iam noctibus?

In this sentence, "illis" refers to something to be done without, "his" to these particular nights (in the ablative plural, agreeing with "noctibus"), and "praesertim iam noctibus" indicating the time in question, which is especially relevant now at night.

Please select the right answer.

 A-Erexi exspectationem tuam, quam vereor ne destituat oratio in manus sumpta
 B-Erexi exspectationem tuam, quem vereor ne destituat oratio in manus sumpta
 C-Erexis quos exspectationem tuam, quam vereor ne destituat oratio in manus sumpta
 D-Erexi exspectationem tuam, quam vereor ne destituat oratio in manerus sumpta

The correct answer is:

A-Erexi exspectationem tuam, quam vereor ne destituat oratio in manus sumpta

In this sentence, "exspectationem" is a feminine singular noun, so the relative pronoun referring to it must also be feminine singular, which is "quam." The phrase "in manus sumpta" means "undertaken" or "taken in hand," which is the correct form.

Please select the right answer.

 A-Quam iuvat quod in tempora illa non incidi, quorum sic me tamquam illis vixerim pudet!
 B-Quam iuvat quod in tempora illa non incidi, quorum sic me tamquam illis vixerim me pudest!
 C-Quam iuvat quod in tempora illis non incidi, quorum sic me tamquam illa vixerim pudet!
 D-Quam iuvat quod in tempora illa non incidi, quorum sic me tamquam illis vixerim pudest!

The correct answer is:

A-Quam iuvat quod in tempora illa non incidi, quorum sic me tamquam illis vixerim pudet!

This sentence means "How pleasing it is that I have not fallen into those times, of which I am ashamed as if I had lived through them!" The relative pronoun "quorum" refers to "tempora" (times), which is neuter plural, so "quorum" is also neuter plural. "Vixerim" is in the perfect subjunctive, matching the counterfactual sense of living during those times, and "pudet" is an impersonal verb in present indicative that refers to feeling shame or being ashamed. The rest of the sentence structure is correct.

Please select the right answer.

A- Is terminus sacri profanique: imsuperiore parte navigare tantum, infra etiam natare concessum.
B- Vis terminus sacri profanique: in superiore parte navigare tantum, infra etiam natare concessum.
 C-Is terminus sacri profanique: in superiore parte navigare tantum, infra etiam natare concessum.
 D-Is terminus sacri profanique: in superiore parte, infra etiam natare concessum navigare tantum.

The correct answer is:

C- Is terminus sacri profanique: in superiore parte navigare tantum, infra etiam natare concessum.

This sentence translates to "That is the boundary of the sacred and the profane: in the upper part, only sailing is allowed, below also swimming is permitted." "In superiore parte" and "infra" are correctly used to describe the locations in relation to the boundary, "navigare tantum" means "only sailing," and "etiam natare concessum" means "also swimming is permitted." The structure of the sentence properly conveys the limitations and permissions associated with the boundary.

Please select the right answer.

1- Nos quidem prompti et parati sumus pro Christo mortem subire, quem morte sua pretiosa nos redemit.
 2-Nos quidem prompti et parati sumus pro Christo mortem subire, qui morte sua pretiosa nos redemit.
 3-Nos quidem illis prompti et parati sumus pro Christo mortem subire, qui morte sua pretiosa nos redemit.
 4-Nos quidem prompti et parati sumus prost christo mortem subire, qui morte sua pretiosa nos redemit

The correct answer is:

2-Nos quidem prompti et parati sumus pro Christo mortem subire, qui morte sua pretiosa nos redemit.

This sentence translates to "We indeed are ready and prepared to undergo death for Christ, who redeemed us with His precious death." The relative pronoun "qui" (who) correctly refers to "Christo" (Christ) and agrees in gender, number, and case, indicating that it is Christ who performed the action of redeeming (redemit).

Please select the right answer.

 1-Si autem heres alicujus talium fuerit infra etatem et fuerit in custodia, cum ad etatet perveneritet, habeat hereditatem suam sine relevio et sine fine.
 2-Si autem heres alicujus talium fuerit infra etatem et fuerit in custodia, cum ad etatem pervenerit, habeat hereditatem suam sine relevio et sine fine.
 3-Si autem heres alicujus talium fuerit infra etatem et fuerit in custodian, qui ad etatem pervenerit, habeat hereditatem suam sine relevio et sine fine.
 4-Si autem heres alicujus talium fuerit infra etatem et fuerit in custodia, cum ad etatem pervenerit, habeat hereditatem suam sine elevio quem sine fine.

The correct answer is:

2-Si autem heres alicujus talium fuerit infra etatem et fuerit in custodia, cum ad etatem pervenerit, habeat hereditatem suam sine relevio et sine fine.

The sentence means, "But if the heir of someone under such conditions is below the age of majority and is in custody, when he comes of age, he shall have his inheritance without relief and without fine." The phrase "cum ad etatem pervenerit" correctly uses the subjunctive mood (pervenerit) to indicate when the heir comes of age. "Sine relevio et sine fine" properly states that the heir shall have the inheritance without any kind of payment or fee.

Please select the right answer.

 1-Nullis distringatur ad faciendum majus servicium de feodo militis, nec de alio libero tenemento, quam inde debetur.
 2-Nullus distringatur ad faciendum majus servicium de feodo militis, nec d'alio libero tenemento, quam inde debetur.
 3-Nullus distringatur ad faciendum majus servicium de feodo militiaris, nec de alio libero tenemento, quam inde debetur.
 4-Nullus distringatur ad faciendum majus servicium de feodo militis, nec de alio libero tenemento, quam inde debetur.

The correct answer is:

4-Nullus distringatur ad faciendum majus servicium de feodo militis, nec de alio libero tenemento, quam inde debetur.

This sentence means "Let no one be compelled to perform greater service from a knight's fee, or from any other freehold, than is owed from it." "Nullus" is the correct nominative singular form to refer to "no one," the term "feodo militis" correctly refers to "a knight's fee," and "quam inde debetur" properly indicates the extent of the obligation.

Please select the right answer.

 1-Curve placent ulli, si sunt in flæribus ora, deperit et lacrymis non decor iste tibi?
 2-Curve placent ulli, si sunt in fletibus ora, deperit et lacrymis non decor iste tibi?
 3-Curve placent ulli, sisuntin fletibus ora, deperit et lacrymis non decor iste tibi?
 4-Curve placent ulli, si sunt in fletibus ora, deperit etelacrymis non decor iste tibi?

The correct answer is:

2-Curve placent ulli, si sunt in fletibus ora, deperit et lacrymis non decor iste tibi?

This sentence could be translated as "Why should any face be pleasing if it is in tears, and does not that beauty perish for you with tears?" The correct Latin spelling for "tears" is "fletibus," and this option correctly spaces the words and uses appropriate punctuation. The phrase "si sunt in fletibus ora" means "if the faces are in tears," and "deperit et lacrymis non decor iste tibi?" suggests that the beauty is not fitting or is lost with tears.

Instructions for Testing
For each error-induced sentence, determine what errors it contains.
• If it contains one target error, and no other errors, select "Target error only".
• If it contains one target error as well as one or more other errors, select "Target error + others". Also
select this annotation if the sentence contains more than one target error (with or without other errors).
• If it contains zero target errors, select "No target error" regardless of whether it contains other
errors.
0 other errors 1 other error >1 other error
0 target errors No target error No target error No target error
1 target error Target error only Target error + others Target error + others
>1 target error Target error + others Target error + others Target error + others
In determining whether something counts as an error, the criterias are below:
Target error for this test: SUBJECT-VERB AGREEMENT
The "target error" is a subject-verb agreement error: a verb (main or auxiliary) that does not agree in person and
number with its subject. Note that this excludes verbs that are improperly inflected to go with a modal or auxiliary
(e.g., I will goes; He will goes; Does he goes).
• Examples of target error:
I goes to the store.
You never sleeps late.
Are it here yet?
He do not have time.
She says that she want to help.
What counts as an "other error"?
Something that is always an error -- i.e., grammatically incorrect – in the given context in generally accepted
varieties of English.
Examples of common types of errors are below, but the types shown below are not comprehensive.
• He got in a accident. (Should be an "an")
• They watched Gone With The Wind Yesterday. (Should be lowercase)
• I wanted to work, I didn't want to go to school. (Comma splice, should be semicolon or have a
conjunction)
What does NOT count as an error?
• Something that may or may not be incorrect, where context is insufficient to be sure.
Example: This march seemed long.
"march" is an error if referring to the month, but correct if referring to an instance of marching.
• Something that is correct in some countries' varieties of English but incorrect or dispreferred in others.
Example: She is in hospital.
"the" is needed in the US but not in the UK.
• Something that isn't stylistically ideal but isn't grammatically incorrect.
Example: He is really, really, really happy.
This is wordy, repetitive, and arguably lacks creativity, but it is grammatically correct.
• Something that is unacceptable in formal varieties but acceptable in somewhat informal (written)
varieties.
Example: Let's all hang out later.
Colloquial wording and use of a contraction are informal but not incorrect.
• Something that is factually incorrect, semantically strange, or potentially offensive.
Example: The dog said he was an idea.
Semantically strange but grammatically correct.

READ DOCUMENT CAREFULLY, I WILL ASK YOU MANY QUESTIONS, CAN YOU DO WHAT I SAID TO YOU ?

Yes, I understand the instructions for testing, and I am ready to perform the task as described. Please present the sentences, and I will determine whether each contains a target error (subject-verb agreement error), other errors, or no errors based on the criteria provided. Go ahead with the questions.

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
Otherwise we risks making visiting another culture on the Web replicate the worst aspects of the insulated tourist experience—many cities in a few days and no interaction with the local society.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence contains a target error in subject-verb agreement: “we risks” should be “we risk.” There are no other grammatical errors in the sentence. Therefore, the correct answer is:

Target error only

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
When is located in a baggage handling area, luggage arrive by the cartload and must be processed quickly.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence contains a target error in subject-verb agreement: "luggage arrive" should be "luggage arrives," since "luggage" is a collective noun typically treated as singular in American English. The phrase "When is located" is also incorrect as it lacks a subject; it should be "When it is located."

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Target error + others

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
Improved health are the goal of care and the longer the hospital-stay, the more exposed patients are to the many harmful bacteria that are found within the hospital environment.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence contains a target error in subject-verb agreement: "Improved health are" should be "Improved health is," since "health" is a singular noun.

There are no other grammatical errors in the sentence.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Target error only

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
Cannot easily view it and have an opinion, judge it, and gain some knowledge of it.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence as provided is incomplete and lacks a main subject, making it a sentence fragment. The auxiliary verb "Cannot" doesn't have a clear subject. To complete the sentence, a subject is needed, such as "One cannot easily view it..."

While subject-verb agreement isn't directly tested here due to the lack of a clear subject, the auxiliary verb "Cannot" would agree with a singular or plural subject once provided, so we wouldn't consider this a target error on its own.

But with the given criteria for "other errors," which include grammatically incorrect elements in the given context, the lack of a subject makes this sentence grammatically incorrect.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Target error + others

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
As accountants with college degrees we is simply better able to demonstrate our educational history.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence contains a target error in subject-verb agreement: "we is" should be "we are," since "we" is a plural pronoun.

There are no other grammatical errors in the sentence.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Target error only

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
During her time in Rome, met many more difficult challenges, however, she were supported and guided by Pope Pius IX.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence contains both a target error and other errors:

Target error: "she were supported" should be "she was supported," since "she" is a singular pronoun.
Other error: The sentence is missing a subject for the phrase "met many more difficult challenges." It should be "she met many more difficult challenges" or a similar construction with a clear subject.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Target error + others

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
What do people mean when they says these issues are soft?

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence contains a target error in subject-verb agreement: "they says" should be "they say," since "they" is a plural pronoun.

There are no other grammatical errors in the sentence.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Target error only

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
Analysts and observers overwhelmingly agreed that Businessland should cut its losses on ComputerCraft and sell off the retail operation.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence contains no target errors as "Analysts and observers" is a plural subject and "agreed" is the correct plural form of the verb to match the subject. There are also no other grammatical errors in this sentence.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

No target error

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
I feels bashed by you.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence contains a target error in subject-verb agreement: "I feels" should be "I feel," since "I" is a singular first-person pronoun and should be matched with the singular form of the verb.

There are no other grammatical errors in the sentence.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Target error only

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
I was down to be with them since one guy had a fully automatic paintball gun, mean we have a good chance of winning.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence contains no target errors in terms of subject-verb agreement. However, there is another type of error present:

The phrase "mean we have" is incorrect; it should be "meaning we would have" or "meaning we had," depending upon the context, to correctly link the clauses and convey the intended meaning.

The sentence contains other errors but no subject-verb agreement errors.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

No target error

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
We reads advertisements to discover and enlarge our desires.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence contains a target error in subject-verb agreement: "We reads" should be "We read," since "we" is a plural pronoun.

There are no other grammatical errors in the sentence.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Target error only

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
Yet, it amazes me how little such research are done in our industry.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence contains a target error in subject-verb agreement: "research are done" should be "research is done," as the word "research" is generally considered a singular, uncountable noun.

There are no other grammatical errors in the sentence.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Target error only

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
Employees are entitled to know where they stands and of any plans that may affect them.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence contains a target error in subject-verb agreement: "they stands" should be "they stand," since "they" is a plural pronoun.

There are no other grammatical errors in the sentence.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Target error only

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
A doctor's visit will likely include a standard X-ray, or radiograph, but unfortunately this method often don't detect minor stress fractures.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence contains a target error in subject-verb agreement: "this method often don't detect" should be "this method often doesn't detect," since "this method" is a singular subject.

There are no other grammatical errors in the sentence.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Target error only

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
Undefined byte codes is distinct from emulated byte codes.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence contains a target error in subject-verb agreement: "Undefined byte codes is" should be "Undefined byte codes are," since "byte codes" is a plural subject.

There are no other grammatical errors in the sentence.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Target error only

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
The two organizations have been working on a merger for about a year.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence contains no target errors in terms of subject-verb agreement. "The two organizations" is a plural subject, and "have been working" is the correct plural verb form.

There are no other grammatical errors in this sentence.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

No target error

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
They creates the Nicene Creed.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence contains a target error in subject-verb agreement: "They creates" should be "They created" (assuming past tense) or "They create" (present tense), since "they" is a plural pronoun.

There are no other grammatical errors in the sentence.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Target error only

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
Similarly, Shipler write about Lisa Brooks, who was also stuck in the infinite circle of poverty; however, was due to a health crisis.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence contains a target error in subject-verb agreement: "Shipler write" should be "Shipler writes," since "Shipler" is a singular noun.

Additionally, there are other errors in the sentence. There is a missing word and incorrect punctuation: "however, was due to a health crisis" should be "however, this was due to a health crisis" or "however, it was due to a health crisis" to correctly introduce the new clause and its subject.

Therefore, the correct response is:

Target error + others

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
That's why I needs your help because some people has put your name as a referee.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence contains several target errors in subject-verb agreement:

"I needs" should be "I need," since "I" is a singular first-person pronoun.
"Some people has" should be "some people have," since "some people" is a plural subject.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Target error + others

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
Most of the people that decide to open up a business back home do not get any source of help, they simply does it and learn through trial and error, as a result the quality gets affected.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence contains a target error in subject-verb agreement: "they simply does it" should be "they simply do it," since "they" is a plural subject.

Additionally, there are other errors in the sentence. There's a comma splice where two independent clauses are incorrectly joined by a comma without a coordinating conjunction: "...any source of help, they simply do it..." should have a conjunction or be divided into two sentences, or use a semicolon.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Target error + others

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
One such approach are through an integrated traffic interface.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence contains a target error in subject-verb agreement: "approach are" should be "approach is," since "approach" is a singular noun.

There are no other grammatical errors in the sentence.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Target error only

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
They is believed to enjoy better earning power than the company's current assets do.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence contains a target error in subject-verb agreement: "They is believed" should be "They are believed," since "they" is a plural pronoun.

There are no other grammatical errors in the sentence.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Target error only

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
If capital budgets is cut, it may affect the transition to 300-millimeter wafers.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence contains a target error in subject-verb agreement: "budgets is" should be "budgets are," since "budgets" is a plural noun.

There are no other grammatical errors in the sentence.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Target error only

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
Would say he could easily get a job running a major industrial country.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence is incomplete: it lacks a subject who would perform the action. A subject is needed before "would say" for the sentence to be grammatically correct, such as "I would say" or "He would say."

This incompletion is not a target error (subject-verb agreement error) because it doesn't involve incorrect agreement between a subject and its verb. It is another type of grammatical error.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

No target error

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
The use of the insert allow the flexibility to create more complex parts without the need for machining, Navarre said.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence contains a target error in subject-verb agreement: "insert allow" should be "insert allows," since "the use of the insert" is a singular noun phrase.

There are no other grammatical errors in the sentence.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Target error only

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
This figure are far beyond the capability of present-day flight-training institutions.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence contains a target error in subject-verb agreement: "figure are" should be "figure is," since "figure" is a singular noun.

There are no other grammatical errors in the sentence.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Target error only

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
And the proactive elements of these practices is delivering outcomes and value.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence contains a target error in subject-verb agreement: "elements...is delivering" should be "elements...are delivering," since "elements" is a plural noun.

There are no other grammatical errors in the sentence.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Target error only

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
Many would say could easily get a job running a major industrial country.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence contains another type of grammatical error: it lacks a subject who would perform the action and it also lacks an auxiliary/modal verb to go with "could." A subject and an auxiliary/modal verb are needed to complete the sentence, such as "Many people would say that he could easily get a job running a major industrial country."

This incompletion is not a target error (subject-verb agreement error) because it doesn't involve incorrect agreement between a subject and its verb.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

No target error

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
The software are expected to be in its test phase by December.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence contains a target error in subject-verb agreement: "software are expected" should be "software is expected," since "software" is a singular, uncountable noun.

There are no other grammatical errors in the sentence.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Target error only

What errors are contained in the below sentence:
You want to quit but you just doesn't know how. Don't know where to go.

 Target error only
 Target error + others
 No target error

The sentence contains a target error in subject-verb agreement: "doesn't know" should be "don't know," since "you" is the subject, which takes a plural verb form even when singular in meaning.

There is also another error in the sentence: "Don't know where to go" is a fragment since it lacks a subject. It should be connected to the previous sentence with a conjunction or semi-colon or presented as a question ("Don't you know where to go?") or with a subject ("You don't know where to go").

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Target error + others
